title: Lumbar Puncture
oneLiner: Bedside diagnostic procedure to obtain CSF for suspected meningitis, SAH, or neuroinflammation; contraindicated by raised ICP or uncorrected coagulopathy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Brain herniation:** raised ICP from mass lesion or severe cerebral oedema – single finding = papilloedema on fundoscopy OR focal neurological deficit (e.g., unequal pupils, hemiparesis) OR GCS <15. **CT head BEFORE LP is mandatory** if any of these are present.
- **Spinal epidural haematoma:** in anticoagulated patient – single finding = INR >1.4 or platelets <50 x 10⁹/L (check local policy). **Do not proceed** until haemostasis is normalised.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (why LP is considered):** fever + headache + neck stiffness + photophobia (meningitis)  
- Thunderclap headache ± vomiting ± syncope (subarachnoid haemorrhage)  
- Progressive paraplegia or sensory level (suspected myelitis or cord compression)  
- Unexplained altered mental state or seizure in immunosuppressed patient  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Kernig’s sign / Brudzinski’s sign (meningeal irritation) – low sensitivity  
  - Papilloedema (contraindication – must CT first)  
  - Focal neurology (suggests mass lesion – CT first)  
  - Rash (meningococcal – may need antibiotics before LP)  
  - Posture during positioning – patient flexed for LP (lateral decubitus or sitting)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Bacterial meningitis** vs **viral meningitis** vs **subarachnoid haemorrhage** vs **encephalitis** vs **demyelination**  
- **Splitter:** CSF xanthochromia (yellowish supernatant after centrifugation) >6 hours from onset → SAH (not meningitis). If in doubt, send for spectrophotometry (UK standard).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Check patient for papilloedema, GCS, focal neurology  
  - Check INR / platelets if on anticoagulation or known bleeding risk  
  - Ensure CT head done before LP if any red flag (NICE: GCS <15, focal signs, papilloedema, seizure, immunocompromise, age >60, known CNS lesion) – but do NOT delay antibiotics >1 hour if suspect meningitis  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Full blood count, coagulation screen  
  - Blood cultures (x2 before antibiotics if time allows)  
  - Serum glucose (paired with CSF glucose)  
  - CRP, lactate, renal function  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT head – first-line if raised ICP suspected  
  - MRI spine – if cord compression suspected (rarely done acutely)  
- **Special tests (CSF analysis):**  
  - Opening pressure (normal 10–20 cmH₂O)  
  - Cell count + differential (polymorphs= bacterial, lymphocytes= viral/TB)  
  - Protein, glucose (calculate CSF:serum ratio; normal >0.6)  
  - Micro: Gram stain, culture, PCR (meningococcus, pneumococcus, enterovirus, HSV)  
  - Xanthochromia (for SAH) – send last few mL in separate tube, protect from light  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head before LP** in any patient with raised ICP red flags – omission risks fatal herniation. Also: **send serum glucose with CSF** – low CSF glucose (hypoglycorrhachia) is key for bacterial meningitis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Ensure correct patient positioning – lateral decubitus (knees to chest, chin tucked) or sitting (for obese patients)  
  - Sterile prep (chlorhexidine 0.5% in 70% alcohol) + drape  
  - Local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1%) – infiltrate skin and track  
  - Use atraumatic needle (Sprotte/Whitacre) to reduce risk of post-dural puncture headache  
  - Measure opening pressure with manometer (attach before collecting fluid)  
  - Collect 3–4 bottles: order as clinically indicated (micro, glucose/protein, xanthochromia, additional PCRs)  
  - Cap and label immediately  
  - If meningitis suspected, give empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g IV) and steroids (dexamethasone 10mg IV) within 1 hour – even before LP if LP delayed  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Bed rest for 1–2 hours (lying flat) – but no evidence that prolonged bed rest reduces headache  
  - Encourage oral fluids (1.5–2 L/day)  
  - Analgesia for headache – paracetamol 1g QDS, caffeine (oral or IV – check local guidelines)  
  - If severe post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) – consider epidural blood patch (administered by anaesthetist)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Follow-up CSF results – switch antibiotics per sensitivities if bacterial  
  - If SAH confirmed – refer to neurosurgery for angiography and management of vasospasm  
  - If demyelination – refer to neurology for MRI and further workup  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h (or per local guidelines)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (post‑LP only if haemostasis normal and no spinal haematoma risk – delay at least 4 hours after LP)  
  - Diet: normal (if no dysphagia or vomiting)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours if stable, hourly for first 4 hours after LP
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetic (for LP):** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL), subcutaneous injection – **max dose 3 mg/kg** (up to 200 mg). Typical volume 2–4 mL.  
  - Do not use lidocaine with adrenaline for LP (risk of nerve ischaemia)  
  - If using lidocaine with adrenaline (elsewhere) – contraindicated near end arteries  
- **Post-dural puncture headache analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/day) + caffeine 300mg PO once (check local availability). Avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or contraindicated.  
- **Alternative:** Sumatriptan 50mg PO once (off-label – discuss with senior).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Paracetamol – no dose change; NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Lidocaine – **exceeding 3 mg/kg** can cause CNS toxicity (seizures, coma) and cardiac arrest. Always calculate max volume before drawing up.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Brain herniation (if LP done despite raised ICP) – **fatal**  
  - Spinal epidural haematoma (especially if anticoagulated) – requires immediate MRI and neurosurgical evacuation  
  - Post-dural puncture headache (most common; 20–40% with cutting needle, lower with atraumatic) – typically positional, relieved by lying flat  
  - Infection (meningitis if non‑sterile technique) – very rare  
  - Nerve root trauma (transient paraesthesia or radicular pain)  
  - Bleeding (superficial or intrathecal)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - CSF leak (persistent headache, low‑pressure symptoms) – may require blood patch  
  - Arachnoiditis (rare, from chemical irritation) – chronic pain
sections.Prognosis:
- PDPH resolves spontaneously in 7–10 days in most cases; ~30% require blood patch.  
- Spinal haematoma: outcomes depend on time to decompression – <8 hours from onset.  
- Meningitis mortality: 10–30% even with treatment – early LP and antibiotics improve survival.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use an atraumatic needle** (Sprotte/Whitacre) – reduces PDPH by >50%.  
- **Measure opening pressure** – rising pressure may indicate hydrocephalus or severe meningitis.  
- **Check INR and platelets before LP** – even in low-risk patients, a quick FBC/coag screen avoids catastrophe.  
- **If you cannot get CSF on first attempt** – withdraw needle completely, reassess landmarks, and try again with fresh needle. Persist only with supervision.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT perform LP without CT head** in a patient with any red flag for raised ICP – you will cause herniation.  
- **Do NOT delay antibiotics** for suspected meningitis while waiting for LP – give empirics immediately if CT or LP is delayed.  
- **Do NOT forget to send serum glucose** – without it you cannot interpret CSF glucose ratio.  
- **Do NOT use a cutting (Quincke) needle** – unless atraumatic not available; PDPH risk is significantly higher.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Headache becomes severe, changes character (worsens when upright, new photophobia)  
  - Fever, neck stiffness, rash – suggests meningitis (even if initial CSF was normal)  
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel dysfunction – suspect spinal haematoma  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review in 2 weeks or sooner if headache unresolved  
  - If meningitis diagnosed – public health notification + contact tracing  
  - If SAH – neurosurgery outpatient follow-up
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - Cannot obtain CSF after 3 attempts  
  - Opening pressure >30 cmH₂O – stop, do not drain – raises concern for raised ICP  
  - PDPH severe after 48 hours of conservative management – discuss epidural blood patch  
  - Any focal neurology or seizure during or after procedure  
- **Call ICU / crash team for:**  
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points  
  - Unequal or dilated pupils (Cushing’s response)  
  - Respiratory rate <8 or oxygen saturation <90%  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any single red score)  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWs2 >5 → escalate to ward doctor urgently; NEWS2 >7 → consider critical care  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if bloods taken) – sepsis concern

rawMarkdown:
## Lumbar Puncture
**One-liner:** Bedside diagnostic procedure to obtain CSF for suspected meningitis, SAH, or neuroinflammation; contraindicated by raised ICP or uncorrected coagulopathy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Brain herniation:** raised ICP from mass lesion or severe cerebral oedema – single finding = papilloedema on fundoscopy OR focal neurological deficit (e.g., unequal pupils, hemiparesis) OR GCS <15. **CT head BEFORE LP is mandatory** if any of these are present.
- **Spinal epidural haematoma:** in anticoagulated patient – single finding = INR >1.4 or platelets <50 x 10⁹/L (check local policy). **Do not proceed** until haemostasis is normalised.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (why LP is considered):** fever + headache + neck stiffness + photophobia (meningitis)  
- Thunderclap headache ± vomiting ± syncope (subarachnoid haemorrhage)  
- Progressive paraplegia or sensory level (suspected myelitis or cord compression)  
- Unexplained altered mental state or seizure in immunosuppressed patient  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Kernig’s sign / Brudzinski’s sign (meningeal irritation) – low sensitivity  
  - Papilloedema (contraindication – must CT first)  
  - Focal neurology (suggests mass lesion – CT first)  
  - Rash (meningococcal – may need antibiotics before LP)  
  - Posture during positioning – patient flexed for LP (lateral decubitus or sitting)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Bacterial meningitis** vs **viral meningitis** vs **subarachnoid haemorrhage** vs **encephalitis** vs **demyelination**  
- **Splitter:** CSF xanthochromia (yellowish supernatant after centrifugation) >6 hours from onset → SAH (not meningitis). If in doubt, send for spectrophotometry (UK standard).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Check patient for papilloedema, GCS, focal neurology  
  - Check INR / platelets if on anticoagulation or known bleeding risk  
  - Ensure CT head done before LP if any red flag (NICE: GCS <15, focal signs, papilloedema, seizure, immunocompromise, age >60, known CNS lesion) – but do NOT delay antibiotics >1 hour if suspect meningitis  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Full blood count, coagulation screen  
  - Blood cultures (x2 before antibiotics if time allows)  
  - Serum glucose (paired with CSF glucose)  
  - CRP, lactate, renal function  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT head – first-line if raised ICP suspected  
  - MRI spine – if cord compression suspected (rarely done acutely)  
- **Special tests (CSF analysis):**  
  - Opening pressure (normal 10–20 cmH₂O)  
  - Cell count + differential (polymorphs= bacterial, lymphocytes= viral/TB)  
  - Protein, glucose (calculate CSF:serum ratio; normal >0.6)  
  - Micro: Gram stain, culture, PCR (meningococcus, pneumococcus, enterovirus, HSV)  
  - Xanthochromia (for SAH) – send last few mL in separate tube, protect from light  
- **Never-miss test:** **CT head before LP** in any patient with raised ICP red flags – omission risks fatal herniation. Also: **send serum glucose with CSF** – low CSF glucose (hypoglycorrhachia) is key for bacterial meningitis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Ensure correct patient positioning – lateral decubitus (knees to chest, chin tucked) or sitting (for obese patients)  
  - Sterile prep (chlorhexidine 0.5% in 70% alcohol) + drape  
  - Local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1%) – infiltrate skin and track  
  - Use atraumatic needle (Sprotte/Whitacre) to reduce risk of post-dural puncture headache  
  - Measure opening pressure with manometer (attach before collecting fluid)  
  - Collect 3–4 bottles: order as clinically indicated (micro, glucose/protein, xanthochromia, additional PCRs)  
  - Cap and label immediately  
  - If meningitis suspected, give empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2g IV) and steroids (dexamethasone 10mg IV) within 1 hour – even before LP if LP delayed  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Bed rest for 1–2 hours (lying flat) – but no evidence that prolonged bed rest reduces headache  
  - Encourage oral fluids (1.5–2 L/day)  
  - Analgesia for headache – paracetamol 1g QDS, caffeine (oral or IV – check local guidelines)  
  - If severe post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) – consider epidural blood patch (administered by anaesthetist)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Follow-up CSF results – switch antibiotics per sensitivities if bacterial  
  - If SAH confirmed – refer to neurosurgery for angiography and management of vasospasm  
  - If demyelination – refer to neurology for MRI and further workup  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h (or per local guidelines)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (post‑LP only if haemostasis normal and no spinal haematoma risk – delay at least 4 hours after LP)  
  - Diet: normal (if no dysphagia or vomiting)  
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 4 hours if stable, hourly for first 4 hours after LP

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetic (for LP):** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL), subcutaneous injection – **max dose 3 mg/kg** (up to 200 mg). Typical volume 2–4 mL.  
  - Do not use lidocaine with adrenaline for LP (risk of nerve ischaemia)  
  - If using lidocaine with adrenaline (elsewhere) – contraindicated near end arteries  
- **Post-dural puncture headache analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS (max 4g/day) + caffeine 300mg PO once (check local availability). Avoid NSAIDs if renal impairment or contraindicated.  
- **Alternative:** Sumatriptan 50mg PO once (off-label – discuss with senior).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Paracetamol – no dose change; NSAIDs – avoid if eGFR <30 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Lidocaine – **exceeding 3 mg/kg** can cause CNS toxicity (seizures, coma) and cardiac arrest. Always calculate max volume before drawing up.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Brain herniation (if LP done despite raised ICP) – **fatal**  
  - Spinal epidural haematoma (especially if anticoagulated) – requires immediate MRI and neurosurgical evacuation  
  - Post-dural puncture headache (most common; 20–40% with cutting needle, lower with atraumatic) – typically positional, relieved by lying flat  
  - Infection (meningitis if non‑sterile technique) – very rare  
  - Nerve root trauma (transient paraesthesia or radicular pain)  
  - Bleeding (superficial or intrathecal)  
- **Chronic:**  
  - CSF leak (persistent headache, low‑pressure symptoms) – may require blood patch  
  - Arachnoiditis (rare, from chemical irritation) – chronic pain

### Prognosis
- PDPH resolves spontaneously in 7–10 days in most cases; ~30% require blood patch.  
- Spinal haematoma: outcomes depend on time to decompression – <8 hours from onset.  
- Meningitis mortality: 10–30% even with treatment – early LP and antibiotics improve survival.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use an atraumatic needle** (Sprotte/Whitacre) – reduces PDPH by >50%.  
- **Measure opening pressure** – rising pressure may indicate hydrocephalus or severe meningitis.  
- **Check INR and platelets before LP** – even in low-risk patients, a quick FBC/coag screen avoids catastrophe.  
- **If you cannot get CSF on first attempt** – withdraw needle completely, reassess landmarks, and try again with fresh needle. Persist only with supervision.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT perform LP without CT head** in a patient with any red flag for raised ICP – you will cause herniation.  
- **Do NOT delay antibiotics** for suspected meningitis while waiting for LP – give empirics immediately if CT or LP is delayed.  
- **Do NOT forget to send serum glucose** – without it you cannot interpret CSF glucose ratio.  
- **Do NOT use a cutting (Quincke) needle** – unless atraumatic not available; PDPH risk is significantly higher.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Headache becomes severe, changes character (worsens when upright, new photophobia)  
  - Fever, neck stiffness, rash – suggests meningitis (even if initial CSF was normal)  
  - New leg weakness, numbness, or bladder/bowel dysfunction – suspect spinal haematoma  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP review in 2 weeks or sooner if headache unresolved  
  - If meningitis diagnosed – public health notification + contact tracing  
  - If SAH – neurosurgery outpatient follow-up

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - Cannot obtain CSF after 3 attempts  
  - Opening pressure >30 cmH₂O – stop, do not drain – raises concern for raised ICP  
  - PDPH severe after 48 hours of conservative management – discuss epidural blood patch  
  - Any focal neurology or seizure during or after procedure  
- **Call ICU / crash team for:**  
  - GCS drop by ≥2 points  
  - Unequal or dilated pupils (Cushing’s response)  
  - Respiratory rate <8 or oxygen saturation <90%  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (any single red score)  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - NEWs2 >5 → escalate to ward doctor urgently; NEWS2 >7 → consider critical care  
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (if bloods taken) – sepsis concern
